taskDescription: Drag the correct anatomical terms into the blanks to complete statements about the posterior triangle of the neck.
textField:
The posterior triangle is bounded anteriorly by the *sternocleidomastoid*, posteriorly by the trapezius, and inferiorly by the middle third of the *clavicle*. Its floor is formed by the prevertebral fascia covering the scalene muscles, splenius capitis, and levator scapulae.

Erb's palsy affects the upper trunk of the brachial plexus (C5, C6), resulting in the *waiter's tip* position. The arm is adducted, medially rotated at the shoulder, extended at the elbow, and *pronated* at the forearm.

The *spinal accessory* nerve crosses the posterior triangle obliquely and is at risk during lymph node biopsy in this region. Damage causes paralysis of the *trapezius* muscle, resulting in shoulder drop and difficulty with arm abduction above 90 degrees.

Torticollis (wryneck) is caused by fibrosis of the *sternocleidomastoid* muscle, causing the head to tilt to the affected side with the chin rotated to the *opposite* side. Early physiotherapy can correct congenital muscular torticollis.

The brachial plexus roots emerge between the *scalenus anterior* and scalenus medius muscles in the posterior triangle. The *external jugular* vein crosses the sternocleidomastoid superficially and drains into the subclavian vein.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the anatomy of the posterior triangle.
Good effort! Review the details you missed.
